layers.name: Compute
layers.status_note: Closed clouds, on-device silicon, and permissionless GPU markets pull three ways

layers.name: Protocols
layers.status_note: Open standards settled under shared governance; newcomers extend rather than fork

layers.name: Discovery
layers.status_note: Platform directories on one side, open registries on the other

layers.name: Identity
layers.status_note: Enterprise IdPs on one side, crypto-native keys on the other, and no standard between them

layers.name: Payments
layers.status_note: The platform-mediated camp fills fast; new agent rails land beside the card networks, not the crypto ones

insights.title: The newcomers picked sides
insights.body: Ten new players this refresh, almost none in the middle. Each reinforced a pole: platform-mediated or permissionless, enterprise or crypto-native. The stack is choosing sides, not converging.

insights.title: Payments is forking, not bridging
insights.body: The new agent-payment rails—ACP, Catena, Skyfire—all land platform-mediated, beside the card networks. x402, Lightning, and Cashu hold the permissionless side alone.

insights.title: Identity split, didn't fill
insights.body: Enterprise IdPs (Entra, Okta, Descope) anchor the closed corner; crypto-native keys (Nostr, UCAN, DIDs) anchor the open one. Production tooling arrived on both sides, but no standard bridges them.

insights.title: Compute grew a permissionless corner
insights.body: Akash, io.net, and Bittensor put GPU supply outside the hyperscalers, open and distributed. The closed clouds still own the centralized corner.
